summary.what: Apply codex-review refinements across six templates after second-opinion sparring
summary.why: An independent second-opinion review surfaced a unifying failure mode — soft fabrication (emotions, themes, consequences, vague benefits) was the same risk in different clothing across the templates — and the targeted patches close those gaps without forcing the templates into stilted compliance.
summary.how: Triaged 14 codex points across 7 templates: accepted 9 (changelog header rule, ADR consequences tightening + date semantics, devblog emotion anti-fabrication + dropped word floor, PR tiny-PR risk override + design-decision collaboration, release-notes incomplete-migration handling + soft-benefit tightening, sprint-report theme threshold + chronology-last-resort, standup stale-next-steps), rejected 2 (weakening devblog literary scaffolding, merging extension-author audience), and bumped versions for templates with material changes.

notes: The codex review's strongest single observation was that I'd been treating fabrication risk as binary (test-plan invention, metric invention) when the softer kind (emotion, theme, vague benefit) is the same failure mode at lower visibility. Accepted that framing fully — every accepted patch is a hardened anti-fabrication clause for a specific soft-content target. Pushed back on the literary-scaffolding suggestion because the named voices (Atwood, Fowler, Orosz) are doing directional work that the LLM actually reads as guidance, not mandatory mood; the right fix for thin-log overreach is the new 'don't fabricate emotion when entries lack it' rule, not blanket voice softening. The codex agent was sparring, not adjudicating — being explicit about what I rejected and why is part of the value of the exercise.
